coredata.dc:title: Water-solvable carbon dots derived from curcumin and citric acid with enhanced broad-spectrum antibacterial and antibiofilm activity
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Fang
Ma, Yurong
Wang, Huibo
Zhang, Mengling
Wang, Bo
Zhang, Yue
Huang, Hui
Liao, Fan
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Multidrug-resistant bacterial infections are one of the most serious public health threats in the world, which bring an urgent need to discover novel antibiotics with enhanced broad-spectrum antibacterial activity. Curcumin, as a widely used natural product, has good anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties; while, the bioavailability, bioactivity and antibacterial activity design are limited by its poor water-solubility. Here, to improve the bioactivity of curcumin, we show the fabrication of highly water-solvable carbon dots (CDs) by hydrothermal method from citric acid and curcumin. The CDs possess excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and antibiofilm activity because they can do damage to the bacterial walls when they get into the bacteria. The bactericidal efficiency of CDs (375 μg/mL) achieves up to 100 % for Gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia coli and Pseudomonas aeruginosa). Additionally, for Gram-positive bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus and Bacillus subtilis), the bactericidal efficiency reaches 80 %. In addition, these CDs also can completely inhibit the formation of biofilm for the Gram-negative bacteria and the Gram-positive bacteria. This work has a significant meaning in studying new antibacterial nanomaterials derived from natural product and the treatment of infections.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Curcumin
Antimicrobial activities
Antibiofilm activity
Broad-spectrum

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program ( 2018YFE0306105 ), National Key Research and Development Project of China ( 2020YFA0406104 ), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51821002 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51725204 , 21771132 , 51972216 , 52041202 ‬), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20190041 ), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province ( 2019B010933001 ), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology , the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) , and the 111 Project .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of CDs. (b) Photograph for the different contents of curcumin (above) and CDs (below) in water. (c) UV–vis spectrum (black line), PL excitation (red line)/emission spectra (blue line) of CDs. (d) FTIR spectrum of CDs. (e) Full XPS survey spectrum of CDs. (f) The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s for CDs.

The fluorescence stability of CDs in different (a) stabilization time, (b) pH values and (c) ionic strengths in NaCl aqueous solution (after Normalized fluorescence intensity). (d) Cytotoxicity of 293 T cells with different concentrations of CDs treatment after 24 h and 48 h.

(a) Photographs of E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus and B. subtilis, after the treatment with different concentrations of CDs (0, 125, 250, 375, and 500 μg mL−1) for 24 h. (b) Bacterial viability of E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus and B. subtilis evaluated with different concentrations of CDs using UV–vis spectroscopy method.
Confocal images of biofilms of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa incubated with or without CDs.
Laser-scanning confocal fluorescence microscopy images of fluorescence, bright field and overlay of E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus and B. subtilis.
SEM images of E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus and B. subtilis treated with and without CDs.
The schematic fabrication of highly water-solvable CDs derived from citric acid and curcumin.

head.ce:title: Water-solvable carbon dots derived from curcumin and citric acid with enhanced broad-spectrum antibacterial and antibiofilm activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Beijing University of Chinese Medicine, Beijing, 100029, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Life Sciences, Beijing University of Chinese Medicine, Beijing, 100029, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou, 215123, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bInstitute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou, 215123, Jiangsu, PR China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Macau Institute of Materials Science and Engineering, Macau University of Science and Technology, Macau, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Macau Institute of Materials Science and Engineering
Macau University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Macau Institute of Materials Science and Engineering, Macau University of Science and Technology, Macau, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multidrug-resistant bacterial infections are one of the most serious public health threats in the world, which bring an urgent need to discover novel antibiotics with enhanced broad-spectrum antibacterial activity. Curcumin, as a widely used natural product, has good anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties; while, the bioavailability, bioactivity and antibacterial activity design are limited by its poor water-solubility. Here, to improve the bioactivity of curcumin, we show the fabrication of highly water-solvable carbon dots (CDs) by hydrothermal method from citric acid and curcumin. The CDs possess excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and antibiofilm activity because they can do damage to the bacterial walls when they get into the bacteria. The bactericidal efficiency of CDs (375 μg/mL) achieves up to 100 % for Gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia coli and Pseudomonas aeruginosa). Additionally, for Gram-positive bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus and Bacillus subtilis), the bactericidal efficiency reaches 80 %. In addition, these CDs also can completely inhibit the formation of biofilm for the Gram-negative bacteria and the Gram-positive bacteria. This work has a significant meaning in studying new antibacterial nanomaterials derived from natural product and the treatment of infections.

ce:section.ce:para: Multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacterial infections have become the greatest public health threats in the world [1–3]. However, the effect of traditional antibiotics is becoming worse and worse due to the development of drug-resistant bacterial strains [4]. This situation has led to an urgent need for the discovery of novel antibiotics and treatment strategies [5,6]. For antibacterials, natural products have been especially important in history, and most antibacterials are root in natural products derived from microbial sources [7–10]. Curcumin, existing in the rhizome of Curcuma longa and in others Curcuma spp, is the main natural polyphenol. It plays an important role in the control and treatment of oxidative and inflammatory conditions, metabolic syndrome, anxiety, and hyperlipidemia [11–15]. Meanwhile, it may enhance recovery and performance in active people by managing exercise-induced inflammation and muscle soreness too [16]. However, curcumin itself has the disadvantages of poor absorption, rapid metabolism as well as fast elimination, which results in its poor bioavailability. Notably, its bioavailability, bioactivity as well as antibacterial activity design are limited by its poor water-solubility [17–19]. Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of new carbon-based materials with good water solubility, low toxicity and biocompatibility [20], which have been widely used in many fields, such as bio-imaging, bio-sensors, antibacterials, catalysis and so on [21–23].

Here, we show the fabrication of highly water-solvable carbon dots (CDs) by a simple hydrothermal method from citric acid and curcumin (Scheme 1 ). Moreover, it was found that the solubility of curcumin changed from insoluble to 2.38 mg/mL with and without treatment. These obtained CDs present excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and antibiofilm activity because they damage the cytomembrane of bacterial during the diffusion process. For Gram-negative bacteria including Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), the bactericidal efficiency of CDs at 375 μg/mL can achieve 100 %. For Gram-positive bacteria including Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis), the efficiency also can reach 80 %. In addition, the CDs can efficiently suppress the biofilm formation for both the Gram-negative bacteria and the Gram-positive bacteria.
All chemical reagents were provided by Aladdin. All bacterial strains come from Guangdong culture collection center (GIMCC).

According to the reported work [24,25], CDs were achieved by hydrothermal carbonization of curcumin and citric acid. 0.2 g citric acid and 0.05 g curcumin were added to 20 mL deionized water and then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The reaction would be heated at 180 ℃ for 24 h and cooled to room temperature afterwards. Most insoluble impurities were removed from the solution by centrifugation and filtration. To eliminate other impurities, the solution was dialyzed and purified with semipermeable membrane (MWCO 100-500). After that, the desired CDs were obtained.

The method of cell counting kit-8 (CCK8) was utilized to test the cytotoxicity of CDs. The 293 T human cell lines (human embryonic kidney (HEK) cell) were cultured in medium (mixed with fetal bovine serum and high glucose at 37 ℃ in 5 % CO2. After 24 h, different concentration of CDs (0, 125, 250, 375 and 500 μg/mL) were added to the cell lines to co-cultivate for 24 h.

All appliances and bacterial medium were used before sterilization. Antibacterial test was done by testing the optical density at 600 nm (OD600) and agar plate method of E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus and B. subtilis. To ensure the antibacterial concentrations, these bacteria are incubated with Luria-Bertan (LB) medium containing different concentration of CDs in the 96-well, respectively. After cultivation for 24 h, OD600 were determined. Additionally, the diluted bacteria suspensions treated with different concentration of CDs were cultured on the agar plate in the constant temperature incubator at 37 °C for 24 h.

The experiment of biofilm formation was conducted with P. aeruginosa and S. aureus. Before the experiments, the bacteria were separately incubated in LB medium in the shaker at 37 °C overnight. The ten-fold dilution of prepared P. aeruginosa and S. aureus suspensions were separately added into the 6-well plates or 96-well plates containing LB medium with different concentration of CDs. After 48 h, the biofilm was formed. The inhibition of CDs on the biofilm formation was evaluated by gentian purple staining method and performed by confocal microscopy.

The morphology of E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus and B. subtilis was obtained by a ZEISS microscopy. First, these bacteria were incubated with or without CCDs at 37 ℃ for 1 h, respectively. After centrifugation, bacteria were fixed in 5 % glutaraldehyde solution overnight. Then, these bacteria were treated with different concentration of alcohol solution (30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 85 %, 95 %, 100 %) step by step. At last, these dehydrated bacteria were resuspended with 100 % alcohol, dropped on a silicon slice and then laid flat to dry in the air. The processed samples were used to test the SEM images after gold sputtering treatment.

In Scheme 1, the obtained CDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method of citric acid and curcumin. The zeta potential of CDs is -15.1 mV. Fig. 1 a describes the TEM and HRTEM images of the obtained CDs. It suggests that the CDs present well dispersion at the nanoscale. CDs also show clear lattice spacing value of 0.21 nm, related to the (100) lattice plane of graphitic carbon [26]. Fig. 1b displays the solubility comparison for the different contents of curcumin and CDs. Different mass of curcumin (0.0125 g, 0.025 g, 0.05 g, 0.1 g and 0.2 g from left to right) was added to 20 mL water respectively from left to right above. And the lower pictures present that different mass of curcumin (0.0125 g, 0.025 g, 0.05 g, 0.1 g and 0.2 g from left to right) was added to 20 mL water respectively from left to right above. And the lower pictures present that different mass of curcumin (0.0125 g, 0.025 g, 0.05 g, 0.1 g and 0.2 g from left to right) with 0.2 g citric acid were treated at 180 ℃ for 24 h. In terms of 0.05 g curcumin, the solubility of curcumin changes from insoluble to 2.38 mg/mL with and without treatment.

The black line in Fig. 1c exhibits the UV–vis spectrum of CDs. At 207 nm, an adsorption peak appeared and was associated with the π-π* transition, representing the carbonyl functional group. The adsorption peak standing for the n-π* transition locates at 280 nm. In addition, the CDs show that the optical emission at 486 nm with excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Fig. 1d reveals the FTIR spectrum of CDs, where due to the stretching vibration of OH, there is a peak at 3448.30 cm−1 [27]. The vibrational absorption signals at 1632 cm−1 could be on account of the vibration of CC and CO [28]. There is an adsorption peak at 1397 cm−1 as a result of the vibrations of CO [29]. Moreover, the hydrothermal reaction was also carried out on the citric acid and curcumin under the same temperature 180 °C. Fig. S1 shows the diameter distribution histograms of CDs, citric acid and curcumin after hydrothermal carbonization. As shown, the particles size ranges from 1.5 to 2.5 nm for all of them.

The element composition and chemical structure of the obtained CDs were investigated by XPS spectra. Fig. 1e shows the full XPS survey scan spectrum of CDs, which was only composed of C and O elements. The Indium (In) comes from the test substrate. Fig. 1f presented the high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s. It's clear from the figure that there are three peaks located at 284.88 eV (CC bonding), 286.38 eV (CO bonding) and 288.70 eV (CO bonding) [30–32], respectively.

To verify the fluorescence stability of CDs, the PL under the influence of different stabilization time, pH values and ionic strength in NaCl aqueous solution were measured (excitation wavelength is 360 nm). As shown in Fig. 2 a, with the increase of the stabilization time, PL intensity of CDs has no obvious change. In addition, under both the acidic condition and alkaline condition (the pH value is from 4 to 10, Fig. 2b), the change of pH value has very little impact on the PL intensity of CDs. Fig. 2c presents that the different ionic strengths has little effect on the PL intensity of CDs, indicating that CDs possess stability in NaCl aqueous solution with different concentrations.

CCK8 assay was exploited to assess the cytotoxicity of CDs by culturing 293 T cells with different concentrations of CCDs (0, 100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 μg/mL) for 24 h and 48 h. As Fig. 2d shown, the CDs at 500 μg/mL develop no obvious toxicity on the cell viability, still around 80 % cells surviving whether the incubation time is 24 h or 48 h. Compared with other toxicity of CDs, it is clear that the obtained CDs possess good biocompatibility and low toxicity [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial activity and antibiofilm activity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of CDs was studied through a series of experiments. E. coli and P. aeruginosa (Gram-negative bacteria) and S. aureus and B. subtilis (Gram-positive bacteria) were chosen to analyze antibacterial effect of CDs, as shown in Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3a, the distribution of the colony on the LB agar plates of four species was presented under the different concentrations of CDs. At 375 μg/mL, the growth of all bacteria was significantly inhibited. Furthermore, different concentrations of CDs solution between 0 and 500 μg/mL were appended into the bacterial LB culture medium. From Fig. 3b, we can see that the survival rate of the four bacteria was close to 0 at 500 μg/mL with the treatment of CDs, indicating that the CDs had inhibitory effects on all the four bacteria. In addition, E. coli and P. aeruginosa in particular, were completely inhibited at 375 μg/mL. For S. aureus and B. subtilis, compared with the other two, CDs show a more obvious bacteriostatic effect, and even completely suppressed the growth of B. subtilis at 375 μg/mL. These results make clear the broad-spectrum antibacterial activity of CDs, and 375 μg/mL is their optimum antibacterial concentration.

Moreover, the inhibition behaviour on the bacterial growth and the formation of bacterial biofilm are two ways to judge antibacterial activity. Bacterial biofilms help protect the bacteria from the effects of antibiotics. S. aureus and P. aeruginosa were chosen as the model of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria to evaluate anti-biofilm effects and perform the follow-up experiments. Culturing S. aureus and P. aeruginosa for 24 h and dripping them on a slice of cover glass were to acquire stable and steady biofilms. When biofilms were formed, CDs were added to co-incubate with them. As illustrated in Fig. 4 , the biofilm formation of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa was effectively inhibited, showing that CDs have high antibiofilm activity on both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria

The fluorescence of CDs was investigated in order to research the antibacterial mechanism of CDs. CDs of concentration of 250 μg/mL were respectively added to E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus and B. subtilis at 37 °C for 1 h. Fig. 5 showed that after incubation, the fluorescence images demonstrate convincingly that CDs can effectively enter bacteria by diffusion.

Next, incubated without and with CDs, the E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus and B. subtilis were cultured at 37 °C for 4 h at 250 μg/mL respectively. By dehydrating samples, the morphologies of the four kinds of bacteria were presented under scanning electron microscope (SEM). As shown in Fig. 6 , the SEM images of E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus and B. subtilis without CDs (control group) showed obvious and coherent membranes of bacterial surface. When CDs was incubated with E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus and B. subtilis, bacterial surface appeared rough and shrunk. A major reason for this phenomenon is the damage of the cell wall. According to these results, it can conclude that the bacterial cell walls can be destroyed. Additionally, CDs can wrap the outer surface of cells, resulting in indirect toxicity and stopping proliferating [34–37].

These results are a clear indication that CDs have antibacterial effects, and their antibacterial mechanism includes the damage of cell membranes. In addition, CDs can effectively get into the bacteria through diffusion [38,39]. The possible reason is that they can affect the function of DNA and protein in cells, thereby inhibiting the growth of bacteria [40,41]. All in all, through these effects, the bacteria was died and antimicrobial effect was achieved

In this work, we show the fabrication of highly water-solvable CDs by hydrothermal method from citric acid and curcumin. It was found that the solubility of curcumin changed from insoluble to 2.38 mg/mL with and without treatment. These CDs have excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and antibiofilm activity because they damage the bacterial walls during the process of entrance. The antibacterial effect of CDs (375 μg/mL) for Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli and P. aeruginosa) achieves up to 100 %, while for Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus and B. subtilis), the efficiency reaches 80 %. In addition, these CDs also can show active suppression on the biofilm formation for both Gram-negative bacteria and Gram-positive bacteria. This work serves a vital purpose: it develops new antibacterial nanomaterials from natural products and the treatment of infections.

Fang Lu: Data curation. Yurong Ma: Data curation. Huibo Wang: Formal analysis. Mengling Zhang: Investigation. Bo Wang: Methodology. Yue Zhang: Methodology. Hui Huang: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Resources, Supervision, Project administration. Fan Liao: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Yang Liu: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Project administration. Zhenhui Kang: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program (2018YFE0306105), National Key Research and Development Project of China (2020YFA0406104), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 21771132, 51972216, 52041202‬), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province (2019B010933001), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), and the 111 Project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtcomm.2020.102000.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Characterization methods, diameter distribution histograms of CDs, citric acid with hydrothermal treatment and curcumin with hydrothermal treatment.
The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multidrug-resistant, extensively drug-resistant and pandrug-resistant bacteria: an international expert proposal for interim standard definitions for acquired resistance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.P. Magiorakos, A. Srinivasan, R.B. Carey, Y. Carmeli, M.E. Falagas, C.G. Giske, S. Harbarth, J.F. Hindler, G. Kahlmeter, B. Olsson-Liljequist, D.L. Paterson, L.B. Rice, J. Stelling, M.J. Struelens, A. Vatopoulos, J.T. Weber and D.L. Monnet, 2012. Multidrug-resistant, extensively drug-resistant and pandrug-resistant bacteria: an international expert proposal for interim standard definitions for acquired resistance. Clinical Microbiol Infect. 18, 268-281.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver nanoparticles: the powerful nanoweapon against multidrug-resistant bacteria

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.K. Rai, S.D. Deshmukh, A.P. Ingle and A.K. Gade, 2012. Silver nanoparticles: the powerful nanoweapon against multidrug-resistant bacteria. Journal of Applied Microbiology. 112, 841-852.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.P. Zavascki, L.Z. Goldani, J. Li and R.L. Nation, 2007. Polymyxin B for the treatment of multidrug-resistant pathogens: a critical review. Journal of Antimicrobial Chemotherapy. 60, 1206-1215.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.B. Sartor, 2004. Therapeutic manipulation of the enteric microflora in inflammatory bowel diseases: antibiotics, probiotics, and prebiotics. Gastroenterology. 126, 1620-1633.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotic resistance and its cost: is it possible to reverse resistance?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.I. Andersson, D. Hughes, 2010. Antibiotic resistance and its cost: is it possible to reverse resistance? Nature Reviews. 8, 260-271.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Trends in antimicrobial drug development: implications for the future

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Spellberg, J.H. Powers, E.P. Brass, L.G. Miller and J.E. Edwards, 2004. Trends in antimicrobial drug development: implications for the future. Clinical Infectious Diseases. 38, 1279-1286.
S. Gibbons, 2004. Anti-staphylococcal plant natural products. Natural Product Reports. 21, 263-277.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Natural products-The future scaffolds for novel antibiotics?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.S. Butler and A.D. Buss, 2006. Natural products-The future scaffolds for novel antibiotics? Biochemical Pharmacology. 71, 919-929.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Natural products as starting points for the synthesis of complex and diverse compounds

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.C. Morrison and P.J. Hergenrother, 2014. Natural products as starting points for the synthesis of complex and diverse compounds. Natural Product Reports. 31, 6-14.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Pereda-Miranda, G.W. Kaatz and S. Gibbons, 2006. Polyacylated oligosaccharides from medicinal mexican morning glory species as antibacterials and inhibitors of multidrug resistance in staphylococcus aureus. Journal of Natural Products. 69, 406-409.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Anand, A.B. Kunnumakkara, R.A. Newman and B.B. Aggarwal, 2007. Bioavailability of curcumin: problems and promises. Molecular Pharmaceutics. 4, 807-818.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Yang, G.P. Lim, A.N. Begum, O.J. Ubeda, M.R. Simmons, S.S. Ambegaokar, P.P. Chen, R. Kayed, C.G. Glabe, S.A. Frautschy and G.M. Cole, 2005. ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Goel, A.B. Kunnumakkara and B.B. Aggarwal, 2005. Curcumin as “Curecumin”: From kitchen to clinic. Biochemical Pharmacology. 75, 787-809.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.K. Maheshwari, A.K. Singh, J. Gaddipati and R.C. Srimal, 2006. Multiple biological activities of curcumin: A short review. Life Sciences. 78, 2081-2087.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Ak and˙I. Gulc, 2008. Antioxidant and radical scavenging properties of curcumin. Chemico-Biological Interactions. 174, 27-37.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Anand, S.G. Thomas, A.B. Kunnumakkara, C. Sundaram, K.B. Harikumar, B. Sung, S.T. Tharakan, K. Misra, I.K. Priyadarsini, K.N. Rajasekharan and B.B. Aggarwal, 2008. sb:title.sb:maintitle: Curcumin attenuation of lipopolysaccharide induced cardiac hypertrophy in rodents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Chowdhury, R. Nimmanapalli, T. Graham and G. Reddy, 2014. Curcumin attenuation of lipopolysaccharide induced cardiac hypertrophy in rodents. Cancer Research and Treatment. 46, 2-18.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent developments in curcumin and curcumin based polymeric materials for biomedical applications: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Mahmood, K.M. Zia, M. Zuber, M. Salman and M.N. Anjum, 2015. Recent developments in curcumin and curcumin based polymeric materials for biomedical applications: A review. International Journal of Biological Macromolecules. 81, 877-890.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.K. Roy, S.M. Kim, P. Paoprasert, S.Y. Park and I. In, 2015. Preparation of biocompatible and antibacterial carbon quantum dots derived from resorcinol and formaldehyde spheres. RSC Advances. 5, 31677-31682.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as potent antimicrobial agents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.L. Dong, W.X. Liang, M.J. Meziani, Y.P. Sun and L.J. Yang, 2020. Carbon dots as potent antimicrobial agents. Theranostics. 10, 671-686.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Applications of N-doped carbon dots as antimicrobial agents, antibiotic carriers, and selective fluorescent probes for nitro explosives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Saravanan, M. Maruthapandi, P. Das, S. Ganguly, S. Margel, J.H.T. Luong and A. Gedanken, 2020. Applications of N-doped carbon dots as antimicrobial agents, antibiotic carriers, and selective fluorescent probes for nitro explosives. ACS Applied Bio Materials. 3. 8023-8031.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In-situ synthesis of Cu nanoparticles hybridized with carbon quantum dots as a broad spectrum photocatalyst for improvement of photocatalytic H2 evolution
